title: Roast Rack of Lamb
subtitle: Herb-crusted French-trimmed rack, served pink
excerpt: A French-trimmed rack of lamb is one of the more elegant quick roasts in the British repertoire. The exposed bones make a striking presentation, and the whole thing cooks in under 25 minutes at high heat. A mustard-and-herb crust pressed onto the fat cap is the classic treatment, giving a golden, crumbed exterior against a properly pink interior.
sourceNotes: Traditional British and French culinary technique for rack of lamb. Jane Grigson, English Food (1974): herb crust method. Classic French-trimmed presentation adapted for British home cooking.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Apply the mustard and herb crust up to 4 hours ahead and refrigerate. Bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before roasting.

glossaryTerms.definition: A preparation where the meat and fat are scraped away from the rib bones to expose them cleanly, giving the rack an elegant appearance and preventing the bone ends from charring during roasting.

content.text: rack of lamb is deceptive in its simplicity. The butcher has already done the hard part; what remains is a hot oven, a quick sear, a mustard-and-herb crust, and about 20 minutes of roasting. The result, when carved between the bones into three-cutlet portions, looks as if considerable effort was involved. Two racks serve four as a main course generously, or six as a dinner party first course.

The internal temperature for properly pink lamb is 57°C to 60°C. At 220°C, a rack of roughly 350 g reaches that temperature in 18 to 22 minutes. Start checking at 15 minutes. A 10-minute rest is important: the thin rack loses heat quickly and the rest allows it to finish cooking gently rather than carryover-cooking too far.

A small roasting pan or a cast-iron skillet large enough to hold both racks without overcrowding. The bones can be protected from charring by wrapping the exposed ends in foil, though at 220°C for 20 minutes they will at most brown at the tips.

Mix {{breadcrumbs-fresh}} of fresh breadcrumbs with the chopped parsley, rosemary, and {{garlic}} of grated garlic. Add {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and season with salt and pepper. Work to a coarse paste. The crumbs should hold together when pressed but not feel wet.

Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan). Season both racks all over with {{sea-salt-flakes}} and {{black-pepper}}. Heat a dry frying pan until very hot and sear the racks fat-side down for 2 minutes until the fat cap is golden. Transfer to the roasting pan, fat-side up. Brush each rack with {{dijon-mustard}} of Dijon mustard, then press the herbed crumbs firmly onto the fat cap and sides, building up an even layer.

Roast for 18 to 22 minutes until the crust is deep golden and the thickest part of the eye reads 57°C for medium-rare or 60°C for medium. The crust should be set and dry; if still soft at 18 minutes, give it 3 more minutes. Transfer to a board and rest uncovered for 10 minutes.

Carve between the bones into individual cutlets, or serve two racks interlocked (a guard of honour) and carve at the table. Three cutlets per person is a comfortable main course; two per person works as a starter.

rows.cause: Mustard layer too thin or crumbs not pressed firmly enough
rows.fix: Press the crumbs hard with the palm and refrigerate for 30 minutes before roasting to set the crust.

rows.symptom: Crumbs burning before meat is cooked
rows.cause: Oven too hot or crust applied too thick
rows.fix: Tent loosely with foil for the final 5 minutes, or reduce to 200°C (180°C fan).

rows.cause: Racks are large (over 450 g each) or oven runs cool
rows.fix: Continue checking every 3-4 minutes. Large racks may need 28-30 minutes.

rows.symptom: No fond in the pan for a sauce
rows.fix: Deglaze with a splash of red wine and a small amount of stock, reducing quickly to a concentrated jus.

content.text: The rack of lamb in its French-trimmed presentation arrived in British restaurant cooking in the 1970s and moved into home cooking during the 1980s, where the herb crust became the standard treatment. The dish sits naturally at a dinner party table: it requires less preparation than a whole roast, cooks in the time it takes guests to settle in, and carves with theatrical ease. Serve with buttered Puy lentils or roasted new potatoes and watercress.
